memories.title: Backgrounded processes in bash hooks MUST redirect stdout/stderr — hook runner waits for output
memories.content:
Production-breaking bug: personality-daemon-start.sh backgrounded a Python process with `&` and `disown` but did NOT redirect stdout/stderr. The daemon wrote FalkorDB connection logs to stdout indefinitely. Claude Code's hook runner waited for ALL output to complete before considering the hook done. Since the daemon never exits, SessionStart hung forever.

WRONG: command </dev/null &
RIGHT: nohup command >/dev/null 2>&1 &

The `</dev/null` only redirects stdin. stdout/stderr file descriptors are inherited by the child process and keep the parent's pipe open. The hook runner waits on the pipe.

This caused Claude Code to be completely unusable — fresh sessions hung at startup. One-line fix but took hours to diagnose because the daemon appeared to start successfully (PID written, process alive).

**Rule:** ANY backgrounded process in a bash hook MUST have `>/dev/null 2>&1` to prevent hook runner from waiting on output.

memories.title: Caught myself duplicating tested logic in untested bash scripts — violated dev flow
memories.content:
When wiring personality hooks into bash scripts, I reimplemented trust forgetting, trait updating, hysteresis guard, and anti-sycophancy cap as inline Python inside a bash script — duplicating logic from the tested Python modules (trust_state_tracker.py, personality_tracker.py, integration.py). This created 3 parallel implementations of the same formulas with only 1 tested.

**Why this is bad:** The clamp formula bug happened twice last session in the tested code. Untested duplicate code is guaranteed to have bugs. Parity check rule violated.

**How to fix:** Bash hooks should CALL the tested Python modules (import and invoke PersonalityHooks), not reimplement the math inline. The bash script is a thin shell launcher, not a Python program.

**How to apply:** Any time a bash hook needs business logic, it must import from the tested Python package. If Python startup time is a concern, use a minimal launcher script that imports and calls one function.

memories.title: PARITY CHECK: When fixing shared logic, find and fix ALL parallel implementations
memories.content:
When fixing a bug or adding a feature to any module that has a parallel implementation (e.g., MCP handler + CLI command for the same pipeline), ALWAYS grep the entire codebase for the same pattern and fix ALL instances.

Failure case: Fixed registryConfidence 0.8 hardcode, auto-discovery, and empty manifest fallback in MCP validate.ts but never touched CLI validate.ts — which is a separate implementation of the same validation pipeline with the same bugs. ChatGPT caught it on re-review.

**Why:** MCP and CLI are parallel entry points to the same logic. Fixing one without the other creates a parity drift that erodes trust and creates inconsistent behavior depending on which path the user takes.

**How to apply:**
1. After ANY fix to a shared concern, run: grep -r "the-pattern" packages/ to find all instances
2. Sherlock review MUST include: "Does a parallel implementation exist? Is it at parity?"
3. Applies to: CLI vs MCP, Azure vs GCP codegen, per-engine dispatch tables, test assertions
4. Never assume a fix to one file covers the same logic in another file

memories.title: Code that isn't wired into the running system is NOT DONE
memories.content:
Code that isn't wired into the running system is NOT DONE.

Violated repeatedly. Latest instance 2026-03-30: corrections.jsonl protocol existed but was never wired into session_end_runner.py — violations were recorded to file but never fed into the trust tracker. The learning system had the violation types, the severity scores, and the record_violation() API all implemented. The wiring was missing.

Fix: session_end_runner.read_corrections() reads corrections.jsonl from the same session dir as events.jsonl. process_session_end() calls hooks._trust_tracker.record_violation() for each entry before running on_session_end().

Before marking ANY feature complete: verify the data path end-to-end. "Written to file" is not "processed by the system."

memories.title: claude -p cache creation costs $0.09+ even with Haiku — never set budget below $0.15
memories.content: Repeated mistake: set --max-budget-usd 0.05 for the LEANN drain script when cache creation alone costs $0.09 (72K tokens). Made the same error 3 times across sessions despite knowing from VAL-005 that cache creation costs $0.09-0.25. RULE: never set --max-budget-usd below 0.15 for any claude -p invocation. Even Haiku with minimal system prompt hits $0.09 for cache creation. The /tmp trick reduces cost but doesn't eliminate the cache creation baseline.

memories.title: ALWAYS use MemoryGraph for memory storage, not file-based auto-memory
memories.content: When storing memories, ALWAYS use mcp__memorygraph__store_memory as the primary store. The file-based auto-memory system (MEMORY.md + markdown files in .claude/projects/.../memory/) is a default Claude Code feature — NOT the persistent memory system we built. MemoryGraph (FalkorDBLite) is the real memory. Use dual_store (MemoryGraph + LanceDB) for important memories. Never default to writing markdown files for memory storage.

memories.title: MCP server smoke test required — import success ≠ MCP server works
memories.content:
RULE: After writing any MCP server, the review MUST verify it actually serves over stdio — not just that Python imports succeed.

**What went wrong**: voice-mcp and archon-monitor daemon were delivered with:
- Passing unit tests
- Passing adversarial review
- Passing Sherlock review
- But voice-mcp had NO FastMCP wiring — `server.py` had plain async functions with a comment "registered at deploy time". The deploy-time wrapper (`__main__.py`) was never written.
- archon-monitor daemon had no `__main__.py` entry point and no launchd plist installed.

Both failures were invisible to import-level checks.

**Required smoke test for every MCP server** (add to dev flow checklist):
```bash
# 1. Verify the module can serve over stdio
echo '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"initialize","params":{"protocolVersion":"2024-11-05","capabilities":{}},"id":1}' \
  | timeout 5 python3 -m src.your_mcp_module 2>/dev/null | head -1

# Must return a JSON-RPC response, not empty output.
# Empty output = FastMCP wiring missing.
```

**For daemon-backed MCP servers** (singleton daemon + session client pattern):
- Daemon needs its own `__main__.py` entry point
- Daemon needs a launchd plist (scripts/packaging/com.archon.DAEMON.plist)
- Plist must be installed AND loaded before MCP session client will work
- Smoke test: check socket exists at expected path after daemon start

**How to apply**: After every MCP server implementation task, run the stdio smoke test as part of the dev flow live smoke test phase. Do NOT sign off until JSON-RPC output is confirmed on stdout.

memories.title: Monitor daemon MUST be singleton with multi-session support — same lesson as personality daemon
memories.content:
The archon-monitor daemon MUST follow the singleton daemon + session client pattern proven by the personality daemon. Key requirements:

1. ONE global daemon process (not per-session, not per-project)
2. Socket at ~/.archon/monitor/monitor.sock (short path — Unix sockets have 104-byte limit on macOS)
3. Each tracked item tagged with session PPID for isolation
4. Multiple Claude Code sessions connect concurrently via the same socket
5. State file uses atomic write (temp + rename) to prevent corruption from concurrent sessions
6. Daemon started by launchd (com.archon.monitor), NOT by Claude Code session
7. MCP server is a thin client that sends JSON-RPC to the daemon socket
8. PPID-based file isolation for session-specific state (same as personality daemon's .persistent-memory/sessions/$PPID/)

The test fixture must use a SHORT socket path (not deep pytest tmp_path — hits 104-byte AF_UNIX limit on macOS). Use /tmp/archon-test-{pid}.sock instead.

memories.title: Autonomous tasks run via LOCAL launchd, NOT remote triggers
memories.content: The autonomous operation system (learning, consolidation, outreach, check-messages, leann-drain) runs via 5 LOCAL launchd agents on 30-min intervals, NOT via Anthropic cloud remote triggers (/schedule). Check status with: bash scripts/archon/status.sh. Check launchd: launchctl list | grep archon. Check last-run timestamps: cat ~/.archon/budget/last-run-*. Budget files at ~/.archon/budget/YYYY-MM-DD.json. Circuit breaker at ~/.archon/budget/circuit-breaker.json. The /schedule skill is for REMOTE cloud agents — completely different system. Never confuse the two.

memories.title: Autonomous learning broken since Mar 25 — 4 root causes found and fixed
memories.content:
Self-learning hadn't run successfully since March 25. Four issues stacked:

1. CIRCUIT BREAKER TRIPPED: 3 consecutive failures tripped the breaker to "open", blocking ALL autonomous runs. Breaker file at ~/.archon/budget/circuit-breaker.json. Fix: reset to closed.

2. BUDGET TOO LOW: learn:max_cost was $1.00 but cache creation alone costs $0.09, leaving little for 20 turns of Sonnet with web search + MemoryGraph. Raised to $2.00.

3. ZSH MANGLES JSON IN $() CAPTURE: This was the critical bug. The runner did RESULT=$(claude -p ...) then echo "$RESULT" | jq empty. Zsh corrupts JSON containing backticks, markdown tables, and special chars when stored in a bash variable via $(). The jq check fails on valid JSON. Fix: write claude -p output to a temp file, then jq parses the FILE directly — never through a bash variable. Changed all 'echo "$RESULT" | jq' to 'jq ... "$RESULT_FILE"'.

4. PERPLEXITY QUOTA EXHAUSTED: /learn skill used WebSearch (routed through Perplexity MCP). Updated skill to prefer Firecrawl with WebSearch/WebFetch fallback for autonomous claude -p runs.

After fix: first successful learn run in 3 days. Cost $0.31, 114s, stored 5 takeaways on hybrid vector+graph architecture. All launchd agents unblocked.

memories.title: Archon packaging: always verify setup-archon.sh before shipping a package fix
memories.content:
Shipped 4 broken releases (v2.2.7–v2.2.10) because I only checked package-archon.sh and assumed the tarball contents would land correctly. Never checked setup-archon.sh.

**Rule:** When fixing anything related to file deployment in Archon:
1. Read setup-archon.sh FIRST — it is the actual install script, not package-archon.sh
2. Read package-archon.sh second — controls what goes in the tarball
3. Verify the tarball with `tar -tzf archon-vX.Y.Z.tar.gz | grep <thing>` before releasing
4. Check git tracking with `git ls-files .claude/skills/` — files must be in git for clone users
5. Only then commit + push + release

**Also:** never commit tarballs to the git repo — attach to GitHub release only. Add `archon-v*.tar.gz` to .gitignore.

memories.title: Personality data persistence fixed — cache files as primary read, MemoryGraph as backup
memories.content:
Root cause: MockMemoryGraph uses key-value lookup but FalkorDB uses full-text search. Hyphenated names like "truststate-current" weren't findable via search.

Three fixes applied (2026-03-29):
1. MemoryGraphAdapter: adds node:<name> tag on store, retrieves by tag. Roundtrip verified against real FalkorDB.
2. session_end_runner: writes personality-session-data.json (trust grade, trait means, mood, approach, narrative) as local cache after processing.
3. personality-start.sh: reads from cache files via jq (fast, no Python needed). Falls back to MemoryGraph only if no cache. Only clears session-scoped files (events, corrections), NOT state caches.

Architecture: dual-write (cache + MemoryGraph), primary-read from cache. personality-start.sh now shows real data instead of "Initializing."

memories.title: LEANN drain failures: client timeout + stale connections holding indexing mutex
memories.content:
LEANN drain script failed all night (40+ times). Two root causes found:

1. Client had hardcoded 5-min timeout but process_queue takes longer for large batches. Fixed: configurable timeout via LEANN_CLIENT_TIMEOUT env var (default 600s). Reduced batch to 10 files.

2. Old index.ts processes from prior sessions connected to daemon and called process_queue. The responses were too large, clients disconnected, but server-side indexing mutex was held by the stuck operations. New process_queue calls queued behind the stuck mutex forever. Fixed by killing all stale index.ts processes and restarting daemon.

Prevention: need to ensure old index.ts sessions don't connect to the new daemon. The .mcp.json now points to proxy.ts but old sessions still use cached index.ts config. Old sessions must be closed or their leann processes killed.

Also: drain script was suppressing stderr (2>/dev/null) which hid the timeout error for hours. Changed to 2>>$LOG_FILE so failures are visible.

memories.title: Autoresearch skill built — Karpathy loop for autonomous skill improvement
memories.content:
Built /autoresearch skill at .claude/skills/autoresearch/SKILL.md implementing Karpathy's autoresearch pattern for Claude Code skills.

Three phases:
1. Setup (human approves): analyze target skill, generate 3-5 test cases, build eval rubric with binary checks, run baseline
2. Autonomous loop (no human): mutate SKILL.md, run tests via separate Agent, score via blind reviewer Agent, keep/discard via git stash, repeat N times
3. Debrief (human reviews): before/after 1-5 rubric scoring, report in .autoresearch/<skill>/report.md, learnings to MemoryGraph

Key design: binary evals (not 1-5) for autonomous scoring — eliminates subjectivity. Execution agent and scoring agent are always separate (blind scoring). Git-based rollback. Plateau detection (5 consecutive discards = stop).

Based on Karpathy's github.com/karpathy/autoresearch (58K stars). He ran 700 experiments in 2 days, found 20 improvements giving 11% gain on an already well-tuned LLM training setup.

Usage: /autoresearch <skill-name> [--iterations N] [--dry-run]

memories.title: MCP servers with relative paths don't work from /tmp — run from project root or use absolute paths
memories.content: LEANN MCP server config uses relative path: src/mcp-servers/leann-search/index.ts. Running claude -p from /tmp means this path doesn't resolve and the MCP tool is unavailable. Fix: run from PROJECT_ROOT for any task that needs project-level MCP servers. The /tmp cost reduction trick only works for MCP servers configured in ~/.claude.json with absolute paths (RocketChat, MemoryGraph). Project-level MCPs in .mcp.json need project root as cwd.

memories.title: MCP Security: OAuth 2.1 + PKCE Required for MCP Servers
memories.content: MCP servers are now formally classified as OAuth Resource Servers (June 2025 spec revision). Authorization belongs to a dedicated auth server. Key requirements: (1) OAuth 2.1 with PKCE (S256 code challenge method mandatory). (2) RFC 8707 Resource Indicators — bind tokens to intended audiences. (3) Client ID Metadata Documents (CIMD) — decentralized client registration via static JSON at HTTPS URL. (4) Token validation must check: signature, expiry, issuer, scopes, and audience. Least-privilege scope negotiation per operation (not per integration). Source: https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/draft/basic/authorization

memories.title: Acronym retrieval gap in vector stores: always use tags for exact terms
memories.content: Vector embeddings are poor at exact matching for acronyms, proper nouns, and technical identifiers (e.g., JWT, MCP, DCAD, API names, service names). Semantic embeddings cluster these with their expansions inconsistently. Best practice in dual-store systems: ALWAYS store acronyms and exact technical terms as discrete tags in addition to embedding the full content. In MemoryGraph specifically: recall_memories handles fuzzy/conceptual queries but search_memories with tags= filter is the reliable path for exact term retrieval. This is an operational gap to monitor in Archon — when storing memories about specific tools, services, or error codes, always include the acronym/identifier as an explicit tag.

memories.title: Agent Protocol Landscape 2026: MCP vs A2A vs ACP vs ANP
memories.content:
Four competing agent communication protocols, each for different deployment contexts:
- MCP (Model Context Protocol / Anthropic): Tool-calling between LLM and tools/servers. 2026 roadmap adds agent-to-agent delegation.
- A2A (Agent-to-Agent / Google): Peer-to-peer agent protocol, 50+ tech partners. Multimodal, opaque agents across frameworks. Supports shared task management.
- ACP (Agent Communication Protocol): Federated, secure autonomous orchestration across organizations.
- ANP (Agent Network Protocol): Open/decentralized agent discovery and communication.

Design principles for autonomous negotiation:
- Support only: accept, reject, or counter-propose outcomes
- Delegated task workflows need: durable state, provenance, idempotency, compensation logic
- Governance belongs in identity, transport, policy, observability layers — NOT only in prompts

memories.title: Hybrid Vector+Graph Memory Architecture for AI Agents (LanceDB + Neo4j pattern)
memories.content:
Current best practice for agent memory combines vector and graph databases:
- Vector DBs (LanceDB): Semantic/similarity search over embeddings. Treats each memory as isolated embedding — fast retrieval, misses relational context.
- Graph DBs (Neo4j): Stores relationships, reasoning traces, conversation context graphs. Three memory types: Short-Term (conversations), Long-Term (facts/preferences), Reasoning (traces).
- Hybrid wins: Vector handles "what's semantically similar?", graph handles "how are these things connected?"

The Cognee framework uses this exact triad: LanceDB (vector) + Kùzu (graph) + SQLite (relational).

LanceDB strength: multimodal lakehouse unifying text, images, video, audio, embeddings — suited for petabyte-scale agentic pipelines with hybrid search (vector + BM25 + reranking).

Limitation of vector-only: misses sequential agent reasoning chains that graph can capture natively.

Relevance to Archon: The dual_store pattern (MemoryGraph + LanceDB) directly follows this hybrid architecture principle.

memories.title: Multi-session isolation fixed: $PPID replaces shared session-id file
memories.content:
The personality-session-id shared file was a race condition — concurrent sessions overwrote each other's SID. Fixed by using $PPID (parent process ID) as the session identifier. Verified that $PPID is consistent across all hook types in the same Claude Code session (all hooks share the same Claude Code parent process).

Architecture: each session gets its own directory at .persistent-memory/sessions/{PPID}/ containing events.jsonl, corrections.jsonl, state.json, interrupt.json, daemon.pid. Shared data (personality-session-data.json) stays in root. Stale session directories cleaned up automatically (>2hr old).

No shared files. No race conditions. No cross-contamination between concurrent sessions.

memories.title: Multi-session isolation: all personality files namespaced by session ID
memories.content:
Multiple concurrent Claude Code sessions were corrupting each other's personality data because all sessions shared the same event/correction/state files.

Fix: Session ID generated at SessionStart (timestamp-PID format). All 7 hooks read the session ID and namespace their file paths:
- personality-events-{sid}.jsonl
- personality-corrections-{sid}.jsonl
- personality-current-state-{sid}.json
- personality-interrupt-{sid}.json
- personality-daemon-{sid}.pid

Shared files (NOT namespaced): personality-session-data.json (latest session's output for next start), personality-session-id (current session's ID).

Backward compatible: if session-id file doesn't exist, all hooks fall back to non-namespaced paths.

Session-end cleans up ONLY its own namespaced files. Other sessions' files untouched.

memories.title: Agent Zero research: extract ideas, don't integrate runtime
memories.content:
Deep research on Agent Zero (agent-zero.ai, 16.4K stars). Python agentic framework with Docker execution, web UI, profile-as-directory agent definitions, dynamic behavior adjustment, persistent vector memory.

Verdict: DO NOT integrate as a runtime. Redundant with Claude Code + Opus 4.6. Separate process, separate memory, doubled API costs, no interop.

DO extract these ideas:
1. Profile-as-directory: agent.md + context.md + tools.md + behavior.md under .claude/agents/custom/{name}/
2. Override-based merging: only specify differences from defaults
3. Dynamic behavior adjustment: agents modify own behavior.md across invocations
4. SKILL.md interop: Agent Zero adopted the same standard

Implementation: Build /create-agent and /run-agent skills natively. Phase 1: minimal format. Phase 2: MemoryGraph + LEANN integration. Phase 3: behavior adjustment + versioning.

Full report: docs/research/agent-zero/summary.md

memories.title: Auto mode works on Max plan — not Team-only
memories.content: Auto mode works on Claude Code Max subscription. The docs say "Team plan" but Max also supports it. Enable with: claude --permission-mode auto (or claude --enable-auto-mode then Shift+Tab to cycle). The classifier runs on Sonnet 4.6, checks shell commands and network ops, auto-approves reads and file edits. CLAUDE.md content is factored into classifier decisions.

memories.title: LEANN drain launchd: full debugging saga and resolution 2026-03-26
memories.content:
LEANN drain launchd agent (com.archon.leann-drain) had multiple issues fixed 2026-03-25/26:

1. Wrong script path in plist (pointed to nonexistent location)
2. macOS launchd sandbox blocks /bin/bash from external volumes — script MUST live on internal drive at ~/.archon/scripts/
3. Script's queue-file check used PROJECT_ROOT which resolved wrong from the internal-drive script location — removed the early-exit check entirely since claude -p has FDA and can access the external drive
4. Even after fixes, the headless claude -p sessions were spending $0.19/batch but NOT actually indexing (process_queue MCP calls weren't persisting). The drain was burning money for nothing.

RESOLUTION: Disabled launchd drain. Ran process_queue directly from active Claude Code session — 1,323 files drained to 0 in ~100 batches. Final index: 1,586 files, 80,020 chunks, 602 MB.

LESSON: Always verify results against ground truth (get_stats) not log output from intermediate processes. The drain logs claimed files were being processed but index stats never moved.

memories.title: MCP 2026 Roadmap: Agent-to-Agent Communication
memories.content: The 2026 MCP roadmap extends the protocol beyond host-to-server communication into agent-to-agent (A2A) communication. MCP Servers will be able to act as agents themselves, enabling multi-agent orchestration natively within the protocol. Key priorities: transport scalability, agent communication primitives, governance maturation, enterprise readiness (audit trails, SSO-integrated auth, gateway behavior, config portability). Source: https://blog.modelcontextprotocol.io/posts/2026-mcp-roadmap/

memories.title: Zep/Graphiti: temporally-aware knowledge graph for agent memory
memories.content: Zep (arxiv:2501.13956) uses Graphiti — a temporally-aware knowledge graph engine — that dynamically synthesizes unstructured conversational data AND structured business data while preserving historical relationships (temporal ordering of facts). Results on LongMemEval benchmark: 18.5% accuracy improvement + 90% latency reduction vs baseline for cross-session information synthesis and long-term context maintenance. The latency gain comes from graph-indexed relationship lookups replacing brute-force vector scans for relational queries. Key insight for Archon: temporal awareness (knowing WHEN a fact was true, not just THAT it was true) is critical for long-running agents — MemoryGraph's importance decay mechanism approximates this but explicit temporal graph edges would be more powerful.

memories.title: Hybrid retrieval pattern: vector + graph with LLM tool routing
memories.content: Best practice for AI agent memory retrieval (per Neo4j patterns): combine vector similarity search with structured Cypher/graph queries, and let the LLM agent route between them based on query type. Rule of thumb: (1) exact relationship questions ("which memories reference X?", "what depends on Y?") → graph queries; (2) semantic/fuzzy recall ("what do I know about authentication problems?") → vector search. Neo4j enables node-level filters (e.g., restrict to Task nodes with specific status), improving precision. Cross-validation via graph structure prevents hallucinations when retrieved vector context is insufficient. Archon's dual-store (LanceDB + MemoryGraph) implements this pattern — use recall_memories for fuzzy recall, search_memories with tags for exact/acronym matching.

memories.title: MCP 2026 Roadmap: Streamable HTTP & Agent-to-Agent Communication
memories.content:
The 2026 MCP roadmap focuses on four areas:
1. Transport scalability: Streamable HTTP for remote MCP servers hits issues with stateful sessions vs load balancers, horizontal scaling workarounds, and no standard for server discovery without connecting.
2. Agent-to-agent communication: MCP servers can act as agents that negotiate and delegate — e.g., a billing agent asks a compliance agent for approval using protocol-level contracts instead of bespoke glue.
3. Enterprise readiness: Gaps in audit trails, SSO-integrated auth, gateway behavior, and config portability.
4. Registry scale: As of Feb 2026, the official MCP registry has 6400+ registered servers. OpenAI and Google both added MCP support in 2025.

Key tool design principle: tools should abstract functionality, not API calls. One tool can encompass multiple API calls to reduce context bloat and cost.

memories.title: MCP Nov 2025 Spec: Async Tasks and MCP Apps
memories.content: November 2025 MCP specification added two major features: (1) Async Task support — servers can start long-running tasks, clients poll for results; Task primitive includes retry semantics and expiry policies. (2) MCP Apps — successor to MCP-UI, allows agents to render interactive UIs (buttons, toggles, embedded web UIs) directly inside host environments instead of plain text responses. This transforms MCP from a data-fetching protocol into a full application runtime. Source: https://medium.com/@dave-patten/mcps-next-phase-inside-the-november-2025-specification-49f298502b03

memories.title: GraphRAG: Multi-modal retrieval combining knowledge graphs + vectors
memories.content: Microsoft GraphRAG (arxiv:2404.16130) builds entity knowledge graphs and community hierarchies from source text, enabling multi-hop reasoning that plain vector RAG cannot achieve. Key query modes: (1) Global Search — uses community summaries for broad/holistic questions; (2) Local Search — entity-based with neighboring graph connections; (3) DRIFT Search — community-contextualized local exploration; (4) Basic Search — fallback to traditional vector similarity. Core advantage: plain vector RAG "struggles to connect the dots" when answers require traversing relationships across disparate information. GraphRAG enables reasoning at multiple scales (entity → community → global). Directly applicable to Archon's dual-store design where MemoryGraph handles relationship traversal and LanceDB handles fuzzy semantic recall.

memories.title: Autonomous runner: always specify A.I.-Chat channel explicitly in check-messages prompt
memories.content: The check-messages prompt must explicitly name the A.I.-Chat channel. A generic "check RocketChat" prompt caused the agent to read #general instead. Fixed by specifying "Check the A.I.-Chat channel in RocketChat" in the prompt. Also fixed: bash 3.2 compatibility (replaced declare -A with case-based get_config function), macOS date %3N not supported (use seconds not milliseconds).

memories.title: Archon can browse web, read PDFs, view images, convert documents
memories.content: Full capability set now auto-approved: Playwright browser (navigate, screenshot, click, fill, type), markdownify-mcp (PDF, images, DOCX, XLSX, PPTX, audio, YouTube transcripts, webpages), WebSearch, WebFetch. Can visually browse websites, read PDF documents with images, convert office files, transcribe audio/video. All permissions in .claude/settings.json.
